summary: 2 categories at High risk driven primarily by the strengthening El Niño. Geomagnetic field shows continued weakening and pole drift but no imminent reversal according to consensus sources.

doomsday_clock.key_reasons: Nuclear risks, climate change, disruptive technologies including AI, and biological threats.
doomsday_clock.assessment: It is now 85 seconds to midnight — the closest in history.

categories.name: Seismic Activity
categories.metrics: 4 M5+ earthquakes in last 24h. Largest M6.1 in remote region. No significant damage or tsunamis reported.
categories.sources_compared: USGS Earthquake Hazards Program + EMSC + GFZ
categories.discrepancies: Counts and magnitudes align closely across sources. No significant divergence on activity level or notable events.
categories.conservative_reading: Activity remains within normal daily variation. Standard household preparedness is appropriate.
categories.prep_priority: Maintain 72-hour earthquake kit. Know drop, cover, and hold. Review local building safety.

categories.name: Volcanic Activity
categories.metrics: Activity reported off the coast of New Zealand with tsunami warnings issued (later downgraded). No other major new eruptions or significantly elevated global alerts in the latest weekly report.
categories.sources_compared: Smithsonian/USGS Weekly Volcanic Activity Report + USGS VHP + local NZ monitoring
categories.discrepancies: Sources align on the NZ event; global activity remains mostly background.
categories.conservative_reading: Regional activity in NZ area warrants monitoring. No immediate global impact from current events.
categories.prep_priority: Residents in NZ coastal areas: follow official tsunami and volcano alerts. General public: monitor local authorities.

categories.name: Solar & Space Weather
categories.metrics: Moderate solar activity. GOES X-ray flux mostly background to C-class. Kp index reached 4. Solar wind speed moderate.
categories.discrepancies: Minor differences in exact Kp peak timing but overall activity level consistent.
categories.conservative_reading: No major geomagnetic storm imminent. Tech operators should maintain normal monitoring.
categories.prep_priority: Satellite operators: monitor for anomalies. General public: minimal direct impact.

categories.name: Ocean Temperatures & Marine Heatwaves
categories.metrics: Niño-3.4 index +1.2°C. El Niño conditions strengthening. 81% probability of very strong El Niño (Oct-Dec 2026 per CPC).
categories.sources_compared: NOAA CPC ENSO Discussion + IRI Columbia multi-model ensemble + BOM Australia
categories.discrepancies: High agreement on strengthening trend and high probability of strong/very strong event. Minor variance in exact peak timing.
categories.conservative_reading: Elevated risk of drought, heatwaves, and wildfire in vulnerable regions over the next 6–12 months. Prepare accordingly.
categories.prep_priority: Water conservation, drought-resistant crops/gardens, heat action plans, wildfire defensible space in at-risk areas.

categories.name: Sea Level Rise & Coastal Extremes
categories.metrics: Global mean sea level rise continuing at ~3.5–4 mm/year. No acute coastal flooding events in last 30 days beyond normal king tides.
categories.sources_compared: NOAA Tides and Currents + University of Hawaii Sea Level Center + CSIRO
categories.discrepancies: Rates consistent across sources. Differences mainly in regional projections.
categories.conservative_reading: Long-term coastal risk increasing. Focus on adaptation and infrastructure resilience rather than immediate crisis.
categories.prep_priority: Coastal property owners: review flood insurance and elevation needs. Communities: support resilient infrastructure planning.

categories.name: Weather Extremes, Drought & Heat Stress
categories.metrics: Active El Niño increasing likelihood of heatwaves and drought in multiple regions. Several heat records in past month in vulnerable areas.
categories.sources_compared: NOAA + ECMWF + WMO extreme weather summaries
categories.discrepancies: Consistent signals on increased extreme heat and precipitation variability.
categories.conservative_reading: Prepare for more frequent and intense heat and drought events in coming seasons.
categories.prep_priority: Heat action plans, hydration protocols, cooling centers, agricultural drought mitigation.

categories.name: Wildfire Activity & Air Quality
categories.metrics: Seasonal wildfire activity within normal range globally. Elevated risk in regions affected by current El Niño conditions.
categories.sources_compared: NASA FIRMS + Global Wildfire Information System (GWIS)
categories.discrepancies: Activity levels align; regional hotspots consistent with drought forecasts.
categories.conservative_reading: Monitor local fire danger ratings. El Niño increases risk in specific regions.
categories.prep_priority: Defensible space around homes, evacuation plans, air quality monitoring (N95 masks for smoke).

categories.name: Military Conflict & Escalation Risks
categories.metrics: Multiple ongoing major conflicts. No immediate signs of rapid global escalation. Continued high tension in key theaters.
categories.sources_compared: CFR Global Conflict Tracker + ACLED summaries + ISW reporting
categories.discrepancies: Some variation in assessment of escalation probability depending on source focus, but overall stable picture.
categories.conservative_reading: Geopolitical risks remain elevated. Monitor for supply chain, energy, and humanitarian impacts.
categories.prep_priority: Diversify supply chains where possible. Maintain awareness of energy and food price volatility.

categories.name: Near-Earth Objects (NEOs)
categories.metrics: No significant close approaches (<0.05 AU) or updated high-impact Sentry objects in the next 90 days.
categories.sources_compared: NASA CNEOS Close Approach Table + Sentry Risk Table
categories.discrepancies: Sources fully aligned on current low risk.
categories.conservative_reading: No immediate impact threat. Continue routine monitoring.

categories.name: Geomagnetic Field Stability & Core Dynamics
categories.metrics: Dipole field strength down ~9-10% since measurements began. North magnetic pole drifting 40-60 km/year toward Siberia. South Atlantic Anomaly has grown substantially since 2014. Inner core relative rotation slowed since ~2010 as part of ~70-year cycle.
categories.sources_compared: ESA Swarm mission data + NOAA/NCEI magnetic models + USGS + recent Nature Geoscience / Nature papers (2023-2024)
categories.discrepancies: Strong agreement on observed weakening, pole drift, and SAA expansion. Some divergence on the degree of concern for near-term full reversal (majority scientific view: low probability on human timescales; process takes millennia). Inner core changes are cyclical and distinct from main geomagnetic reversal.
categories.conservative_reading: No evidence supports an imminent full geomagnetic reversal. Primary practical concerns are increased radiation exposure in the SAA for satellites and high-latitude aviation. Continue monitoring official models (WMM, IGRF).
categories.prep_priority: Satellite and aviation operators: radiation hardening and monitoring protocols. General public: awareness that GPS/navigation can be affected during strong solar events (already covered in Solar category).

astronomical_context.note: Earth's mean solar day shows long-term lengthening trend (~1.7 ms/century due to tidal friction) with short-term fluctuations from core dynamics and climate effects. Recent inner core studies show cyclical variation. No leap second scheduled imminently.
